plain_english_takeaway: Example single-cell motor speed trace showing rapid slowdown upon sucrose shock and partial recovery.
Sucrose shock rapidly slows the flagellar motor, and larger shocks cause larger slowdowns.
Total normalized speed decrease scales linearly with change in osmolarity for sucrose shocks.
Viscosity-corrected speed decrease also scales linearly with osmolarity showing PMF-driven component.
TMRM fluorescence drops after shock, independently supporting membrane depolarization.
A different non-ionic osmolyte causes a similar motor slowdown, so the effect is not sucrose-specific.
The response persists without potassium in the buffer, so immediate potassium uptake is not required.
Clockwise-locked motors slow similarly, pointing upstream of motor direction switching.
During longer shock, motor speed partly recovers over minutes, consistent with adaptation and PMF restoration.
Cells shrink during osmotic shock and recover after return to base media.
Measured sorbitol motor speed can be compared with a viscosity-only expectation.
Clockwise motor speed compared with viscosity-only expectation as a control.
Representative individual motor speed trace for 200 mM sucrose shock.

source_data: data/time-series/bead/sucrose_*.parquet; data/time-series/bead/sorbitol_*.parquet; data/Osmolarity-readings/sucrose.xlsx; data/Osmolarity-readings/sorbitol.xlsx; data/Viscosity/Sucro/; data/Viscosity/Sorb/
plain_english_takeaway: Viscosity-corrected speed decrease is identical for sucrose and sorbitol confirming a common cellular response.
Timescale of motor speed decrease during sucrose shock does not depend on shock strength.
Timescale of motor speed recovery after sucrose shock does not depend on shock strength.
Measured sucrose motor speed compared with a viscosity-only prediction to isolate PMF-driven component.
